records.name: SRA Transparency Rules 2018 (price and service publication, complaints, regulatory information and digital badge)

citation.act: SRA Transparency Rules 2018 (made under s.31 and s.156 Legal Services Act 2007; part of the SRA Standards and Regulations)
citation.section: Rule 1 (cost and service information), Rule 2 (complaints information), Rule 3 (VAT/disbursements), Rule 4 (regulatory information and SRA digital badge)

applies_when: firm is authorised and regulated by the SRA (recognised body, recognised sole practice, or licensed body/ABS)
firm offers any of the in-scope services to the public or to businesses (see Rule 1 list)
excluded_when: not an SRA-regulated firm (barristers, licensed conveyancers, CILEx firms are covered by their own regulators)
publicly funded work is out of scope for the PRICE obligation only (the complaints, regulatory and badge duties still apply)
personal injury work is NOT an in-scope service and does not trigger the price-publication duty (E26)

website_obligations.duty: Publish cost information for each in-scope service the firm offers, on the website in a prominent place
elements: individuals: residential conveyancing (freehold/leasehold sale, purchase, mortgage/remortgage)
individuals: uncontested probate where all assets are in the UK
individuals: immigration (excluding asylum), including First-tier Tribunal appeals
individuals: motoring offences that are summary only, single Magistrates' Court hearing
individuals: employment tribunal claims for unfair or wrongful dismissal (claimant/employee side)
businesses: employment tribunal defence of unfair or wrongful dismissal (employer side)
businesses: debt recovery up to the value of GBP 100,000
businesses: licensing applications for business premises

for each: total or average cost or a realistic range, the basis of charges, likely disbursements and their cost, whether VAT is included, key stages and typical timescales, and the experience/qualifications of those doing the work

website_obligations.duty: Publish the firm's complaints handling procedure including how and when a complaint can be made to the Legal Ombudsman and to the SRA (Rule 2)
elements: complaints procedure findable on the website
Legal Ombudsman route and contact named
SRA route named for conduct concerns

website_obligations.duty: Display regulatory information: the statement that the firm is authorised and regulated by the Solicitors Regulation Authority and the firm's SRA number (Rule 4)
elements: 'authorised and regulated by the Solicitors Regulation Authority' wording present
SRA firm number shown

website_obligations.duty: Display the clickable SRA digital badge (SRA-issued verification badge) in a prominent place (Rule 4)
elements: SRA digital badge embedded and linking to the SRA verification page

penalty.basis: SRA fixed penalties for transparency breaches begin at GBP 750 and escalate to GBP 1,500 on continued non-compliance; the SRA's internal financial-penalty cap for traditional firms and individuals is GBP 25,000 (from 20 July 2022). Licensed bodies/ABS and referral to the Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal carry far higher or unlimited fines; a headline figure such as GBP 2.6m is an SDT/court outcome, not an SRA-imposed transparency fine (E27).

enforcement.case: SRA fixed penalties for Transparency Rules non-compliance (multiple firms)
enforcement.summary: The SRA issued fixed penalties to firms that failed to publish price information or diversity data, part of an ongoing programme of website transparency sweeps.

intel.why_matters: The SRA runs recurring automated and manual website sweeps against the exact in-scope service list; a firm that offers conveyancing, probate or immigration but has no compliant price page is an easy fixed-penalty target.
intel.regulator_asks_first: For each in-scope service you advertise, is there a price page with costs, basis, disbursements, VAT treatment and timescales, plus a complaints procedure naming the Legal Ombudsman, your SRA number and the digital badge?
intel.relevance_hook: A missing or thin price page loses both the SRA sweep and the client who compares three firms before enquiring; the same page fixes both.

records.name: SRA Code of Conduct - client information, publicity and unsolicited approaches (Section 8)

citation.act: SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs; SRA Code of Conduct for Firms (SRA Standards and Regulations)
citation.section: para 8.8 (publicity accurate and not misleading), 8.9 (no unsolicited approaches to the public), 8.10 (clients understand whether/how services are regulated), 8.11 (comply with the Transparency Rules)

applies_when: firm or solicitor authorised by the SRA publishes marketing, practice-area or results content on a website
excluded_when: not an SRA-regulated person or firm

website_obligations.duty: Ensure all website publicity is accurate and not misleading, including statements about charges and about interest (para 8.8)
elements: no unsubstantiated success-rate, 'win', 'guaranteed outcome' or 'best/leading' claims
fee and interest statements accurate
no misleading 'no win no fee' framing that hides deductions or exclusions

website_obligations.duty: Do not present the website as making unsolicited approaches to members of the public beyond current or former clients (para 8.9)
elements: marketing does not solicit prohibited direct approaches in a way that breaches 8.9

website_obligations.duty: Make clear on the site whether and how the services offered are regulated and which activities are carried out as an authorised person (para 8.10)
elements: regulated status explained
distinction drawn where some services are not reserved/regulated legal activities

penalty.basis: SRA internal financial penalty up to GBP 25,000 for traditional firms and individuals (unlimited where the failure relates to economic crime, from 4 March 2024); serious cases are referred to the Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal, which can impose unlimited fines, suspension or strike-off. Licensed bodies/ABS face higher SRA caps.

enforcement.case: SRA warning notice on misleading no-win-no-fee and outcome marketing
enforcement.summary: The SRA warned firms about misleading no-win-no-fee marketing and unsubstantiated claims, reinforcing that website publicity must be accurate under the Code.

intel.why_matters: Practice-area and results pages are exactly where firms over-claim; the SRA treats a misleading website as a Code breach in its own right, independent of any client complaint.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can every superlative, success-rate or 'guaranteed' claim on the site be substantiated, and is the regulated status of each service made clear?
intel.relevance_hook: The words that convert (best, leading, guaranteed, 100% success) are the words the SRA sanctions; disciplined copy protects both trust and registration.

records.name: Legal Ombudsman signposting in complaints handling (SRA Code paras 8.3-8.4 and LeO Scheme Rules)

citation.act: SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs paras 8.3-8.4; Legal Ombudsman Scheme Rules
citation.section: SRA Code 8.3 (tell clients at engagement of their right to complain to the Legal Ombudsman and when), 8.4 (written signpost after 8 weeks unresolved with the time frame and contact details); LeO Scheme Rules time limits

applies_when: a regulated legal services provider handles a client complaint about its service
excluded_when: complainant is outside the Legal Ombudsman's jurisdiction (e.g. certain large businesses)

website_obligations.duty: Publish complaints information that signposts the Legal Ombudsman with the correct time limits and contact details
elements: Legal Ombudsman named as the escalation route with postal address, phone and website
the client may refer within six months of the firm's final written response
the overall LeO time limit stated (referral within one year of the act/omission, or one year from when the client should reasonably have known)
statement that the firm's final response must explain the LeO route and the six-month deadline for the deadline to bite

penalty.basis: Failure to signpost is a conduct breach handled by the relevant regulator (SRA up to GBP 25,000 for traditional firms/individuals, or SDT referral). A defective final response that omits the six-month warning also means the LeO time limit never starts, so stale complaints remain live against the firm.

regulator.name: Legal Ombudsman (and the firm's front-line regulator: SRA/BSB/CLC)

intel.why_matters: If the final response letter omits the Legal Ombudsman signpost and the six-month deadline, the deadline never runs, so a client can escalate months or years later; the website complaints page is the cheapest place to get this right for every matter.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the complaints page name the Legal Ombudsman with contact details and both time limits, and do final responses carry the six-month warning?
intel.relevance_hook: A one-paragraph complaints block, correctly worded, closes off open-ended Ombudsman exposure and satisfies the regulator sweep at the same time.

records.name: Legal Services Act 2007 - authorisation to carry on reserved legal activities

citation.section: s.12-15 (reserved legal activities and authorisation), s.14 (offence to carry on a reserved activity when not entitled), s.16-17 (offence to pretend to be entitled)

applies_when: the provider offers a reserved legal activity: rights of audience, conduct of litigation, reserved instrument activities (conveyancing), probate activities, notarial activities, or the administration of oaths
excluded_when: only unreserved legal work is offered (e.g. general legal advice, will-writing, employment advice), for which no authorisation is required

website_obligations.duty: Where the site advertises a reserved legal activity, the provider must in fact be authorised and should make its regulated status and regulator visible
elements: named regulator (SRA/BSB/CLC/CILEx/Faculty Office) shown
no advertising of reserved activities the provider is not authorised to carry on
no false claim or implication of being a solicitor/barrister/authorised person

penalty.basis: Carrying on a reserved legal activity while not entitled, or pretending to be entitled, is a criminal offence under s.14 and s.16-17: on indictment an unlimited fine and/or up to two years' imprisonment. No civil tariff.

regulator.name: Legal Services Board; front-line regulators SRA/BSB/CLC/CILEx Regulation/Faculty Office

intel.why_matters: A website that advertises litigation, court advocacy, conveyancing or probate implicitly asserts authorisation; if the entity is not authorised for that reserved activity the advertising itself is evidence of a criminal offence.
intel.regulator_asks_first: For every reserved activity the site advertises, is the entity actually authorised and is its regulator named?
intel.relevance_hook: Unreserved firms (will-writers, McKenzie friends, consultancies) get this wrong constantly; the fix is precise wording that stays inside what they are entitled to offer.

records.name: BSB Handbook - barrister and chambers publicity and Public Access disclosure

citation.act: Bar Standards Board Handbook (Code of Conduct)
citation.section: rC19-rC21 (not misleading the public; accurate practice information; no false/misleading claims of specialism); Public Access rules and Public Access guidance

applies_when: self-employed barrister, chambers or BSB entity publishes a website or profile, or offers Public Access (direct access) work
excluded_when: not a BSB-regulated person or entity

website_obligations.duty: Ensure practice and profile information is not misleading and does not make unsubstantiated superiority or specialism claims (rC19-rC21)
elements: no misleading seniority, ranking or success claims on tenant profiles
accurate description of areas of practice and status
BSB-regulated status identifiable

website_obligations.duty: Where Public Access / direct access work is offered, disclose the Public Access basis and the client's position clearly
elements: Public Access availability and scope stated
explanation that a solicitor is not required for Public Access instructions where applicable
complaints route to the Legal Ombudsman shown

penalty.basis: BSB brings disciplinary proceedings; serious cases are referred to the Bar Tribunals and Adjudication Service (BTAS), which can impose fines, suspension or disbarment. No fixed statutory tariff is published for these website breaches; do not state a specific cap.

intel.why_matters: Chambers websites carry tenant profiles written by the barristers themselves, and superiority or ranking claims that cannot be substantiated are a direct rC19-rC21 breach.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the practice descriptions and any specialism/ranking claims accurate and substantiated, and is Public Access scope disclosed where offered?
intel.relevance_hook: The profile copy that wins instructions is the same copy the BSB scrutinises; disciplined, accurate profiles protect both.

records.name: Bar Transparency Rules - price, service and redress information for barristers and chambers

citation.act: Bar Standards Board Handbook - Transparency Rules (in force 1 July 2019, following the CMA legal services market study)
citation.section: BSB Transparency Rules (mandatory price, service and redress information); BSB Transparency Standards Guidance

applies_when: self-employed barristers, chambers and BSB entities that have a website
especially those offering Public Access and the specified services within the price-transparency scope
excluded_when: no website (but the information must then be available in an alternative format such as a factsheet on request)

website_obligations.duty: Publish price, service and redress information on the website
elements: most commonly used pricing models (e.g. fixed fee or hourly rate)
areas of practice and description of the most commonly provided legal services
factors that might influence timescales
the prescribed regulatory text confirming BSB regulation
complaints procedure and the right to complain to the Legal Ombudsman, plus decision data where required

penalty.basis: Non-compliance is enforced by the BSB through supervision and, for serious cases, disciplinary referral to BTAS. No fixed statutory fine tariff is published for these website breaches; do not assert a specific cap.

intel.why_matters: Chambers frequently treat the Bar Transparency Rules as satisfied by a single generic page; the BSB ran compliance programmes precisely because so many sites lacked pricing models, timescale factors and the mandatory regulatory text.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site show pricing models, service descriptions, timescale factors, the BSB regulatory statement and the complaints/Legal Ombudsman route?
intel.relevance_hook: A compliant transparency page also answers the exact questions a lay Public Access client asks before instructing, so it converts as well as it complies.

records.name: CLC website transparency - price, service and complaints information for licensed conveyancers

citation.act: Council for Licensed Conveyancers (CLC) Handbook - Transparency (Cost and Service) Framework and CLC Code of Conduct
citation.section: CLC transparency requirements (cost information, service information, CLC digital badge, complaints and Legal Ombudsman route)

applies_when: practice regulated by the Council for Licensed Conveyancers offers conveyancing or probate services
excluded_when: not CLC-regulated (SRA firms follow the SRA Transparency Rules instead)

website_obligations.duty: Display cost information in a prominent place on the website
elements: prices or price ranges for conveyancing/probate
basis of charges and likely disbursements
VAT treatment

website_obligations.duty: Display service information and the CLC regulatory badge
elements: description of the service and key stages/timescales
CLC secure/verification badge shown prominently
statement that the practice is regulated by the CLC

website_obligations.duty: Display the complaints process including access to the Legal Ombudsman and redress information
elements: complaints procedure published
Legal Ombudsman route and contact named

penalty.basis: Enforced by the CLC through its disciplinary and enforcement powers; sanctions include conditions, fines and licence revocation. No single fixed statutory website tariff is published; do not assert a specific figure.

intel.why_matters: CLC firms compete head-to-head with SRA firms on the same conveyancing keywords, and the CLC mandates the same cost/service/redress transparency; a firm that shows neither price nor badge loses the comparison and the CLC check.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is cost information prominent, is the CLC badge shown, and is the complaints/Legal Ombudsman route published?
intel.relevance_hook: Conveyancing buyers price-shop; the compliant cost page is also the highest-converting page on a conveyancer's site.

records.name: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 - AML supervision (legal sector)

citation.act: The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (SI 2017/692)
citation.section: reg 8-9 (relevant persons and supervision), reg 18-21 (risk assessment, policies and controls), reg 27-38 (customer due diligence); Part 7 Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 for the underlying offences

applies_when: firm carries on in-scope legal activity (e.g. conveyancing, trust or company formation, managing client money, certain tax/transactional work) and is therefore a 'relevant person' supervised for AML
excluded_when: firm carries on only work outside the MLR 2017 regulated sectors (much litigation and general advice is out of scope)

website_obligations.duty: Where the firm holds itself out as AML-supervised, name the correct supervisor (for most solicitors this is the SRA, not HMRC) so the claim is accurate; do not overstate the supervision
elements: AML supervisor correctly identified where stated (SRA for solicitors; CLC for licensed conveyancers)
no claim of AML supervision the firm does not in fact hold

penalty.basis: Breach of the MLR 2017 can lead to civil penalties from the supervisor and, for the underlying money-laundering offences under POCA 2002, an unlimited fine and up to 14 years' imprisonment. The website nexus is narrow: the site is mainly evidence of an accurate (or overstated) supervision claim, not the primary breach.

regulator.name: Solicitors Regulation Authority (AML supervisor for most solicitors); HM Treasury/OPBAS oversight; HMRC for some sectors

intel.why_matters: Most solicitors are supervised for AML by the SRA, yet firms sometimes state 'HMRC supervised' on the website, which is inaccurate and flags weak AML control.
intel.regulator_asks_first: If the site mentions AML supervision, does it name the correct supervisor for this firm type?
intel.relevance_hook: This is a small wording fix that removes an easy credibility and accuracy flag, though the substantive MLR duties are operational rather than web-facing.

records.name: Provision of Services Regulations 2009 - professional service-provider information duties

citation.section: reg 8 (information a provider must make available), reg 9 (further information on request), reg 11 (professional liability insurance information where the provider is required to hold it)

applies_when: a service provider (including a legal or professional-services firm) supplies services to recipients in the UK
excluded_when: activities outside the scope of the Regulations (e.g. certain financial services, healthcare and other carve-outs listed in the Regulations)

website_obligations.duty: Make available to recipients the core provider information (typically via the website)
elements: name of the provider and legal status/form
geographic (postal) address and contact details including an email or other means for complaints and enquiries
VAT number where the provider is VAT registered
where a regulated profession: the professional body/register, the professional title and the state that granted it, plus a reference to the applicable professional rules and how to access them
the main features of the service and the price where pre-determined
general terms and conditions if the provider uses them

website_obligations.duty: Where the provider is required to hold professional liability (PI) insurance, make available details of that insurance
elements: professional indemnity insurance details or coverage territory available on request or on the site where required

penalty.basis: Enforced by trading standards / weights and measures authorities and via the courts; the Regulations work through enforcement orders and consumer redress rather than a fixed statutory fine tariff. Do not assert a specific penalty figure.

regulator.name: Local authority trading standards (Provision of Services Regulations enforcement)

intel.why_matters: These are the baseline 'who we are and how we are regulated' disclosures that every professional-services website should carry, and legal firms are explicitly in scope; the Bar Council and Law Society both publish checklists against these Regulations.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site show the provider's name, legal status, address, VAT number, professional body and rules, and PI insurance details where required?
intel.relevance_hook: One well-built legal/regulatory footer block satisfies most of these duties at once and signals a serious, credible firm.

records.name: Companies Act 2006 s.82 and Company, LLP and Business (Names and Trading Disclosures) Regulations - website trading disclosures

citation.act: Companies Act 2006 s.82; The Company, Limited Liability Partnership and Business (Names and Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/17)
citation.section: CA 2006 s.82; SI 2015/17 regs on disclosure of registered particulars on websites and business communications

applies_when: the legal practice is an incorporated entity (limited company) or an LLP with a website or business communications
excluded_when: traditional partnership or sole practitioner not incorporated (different, lighter disclosure applies)

website_obligations.duty: Display the registered particulars on the website and business communications
elements: registered company or LLP name (as on the register)
company/LLP registration number
place of registration (e.g. Registered in England and Wales)
registered office address

penalty.basis: Failure to make required trading disclosures is a criminal offence; a fine up to level 3 on the standard scale (GBP 1,000) for the company/LLP and every officer in default, with a daily default fine for continued contravention.

regulator.name: Companies House / local authority trading standards

intel.why_matters: Law firms are overwhelmingly LLPs or limited companies, yet many display only a trading name; the registered name, number, place of registration and registered office are a legal requirement and are trivially checkable against Companies House.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the footer show the registered name, company/LLP number, place of registration and registered office?
intel.relevance_hook: A single compliant disclosure block in the footer fixes this site-wide in a day and reinforces the firm's legitimacy.

records.name: SRA workforce diversity data collection and publication

citation.act: SRA Standards and Regulations - collection and publication of diversity data (arising from the Legal Services Act 2007 s.149 regulatory objective on diversity)
citation.section: SRA requirement to collect workforce diversity data every two years and publish it (with the SRA reporting aggregate outcomes)

applies_when: firm is authorised and regulated by the SRA

website_obligations.duty: Collect workforce diversity data and publish it (e.g. on the website) as the SRA requires
elements: diversity data collected from the workforce
aggregated diversity information published/made available in line with SRA guidance

penalty.basis: The SRA has issued fixed penalties (from GBP 750) to firms that failed to report diversity data; wider conduct action can follow, subject to the SRA's GBP 25,000 cap for traditional firms/individuals.

enforcement.case: SRA first fixed penalty for failure to submit diversity data
enforcement.summary: The SRA issued its first fixed penalty for a firm failing to submit its diversity data, alongside transparency-rules fixed penalties.

intel.why_matters: Diversity-data publication is a distinct, easily-missed SRA obligation that has already produced fixed penalties, separate from the pricing transparency sweeps.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Has the firm collected and published its workforce diversity data as the SRA requires?
intel.relevance_hook: A short diversity statement page closes a live fixed-penalty exposure that most firms overlook.

records.name: CILEx Regulation transparency - price and service information for CILEx-regulated practices

citation.act: CILEx Regulation (CRL) Code of Conduct and transparency requirements
citation.section: CILEx Regulation transparency requirements on price, service and complaints information for authorised entities and individuals

applies_when: practice or practitioner authorised by CILEx Regulation offers legal services to the public
excluded_when: not CILEx-regulated (SRA/CLC firms follow their own regulators' rules)

website_obligations.duty: Publish price, service and complaints information in line with CILEx Regulation transparency requirements
elements: price/cost information for the services offered
description of services and timescales
complaints procedure and Legal Ombudsman route
CILEx Regulation regulated status shown

penalty.basis: Enforced by CILEx Regulation through supervision and disciplinary action. No fixed statutory website tariff is published; do not assert a specific figure.

intel.why_matters: CILEx-regulated practices are held to transparency requirements comparable to the SRA and CLC, but firms often assume the rules do not reach them.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site publish price and service information, the complaints/Legal Ombudsman route, and the CILEx Regulation status?
intel.relevance_hook: A compliant transparency page matches the SRA/CLC competitors these firms sit next to in search results.
